articles.title: MOST ENTERPRISE AI APPS FAIL. HERE'S WHAT WORKS.
articles.summary: Most AI apps never make it past the prototype phase because of compliance gaps, security issues, and weak infrastructure. This guide outlines how successful teams move quickly from idea to launch.

articles.title: CLAY CONFIRMS IT CLOSED $100M ROUND AT $3.1B VALUATION
articles.summary: Clay secured a $100 million Series C at a $3.1 billion valuation, led by CapitalG, confirming earlier reports. This funding follows significant rounds, including a $1.25 billion Series B six months ago. With prominent clients like OpenAI and Canva, Clay expects to triple last year's revenue to $100 million by year-end.

articles.title: SMALL ACTION MODELS ARE THE FUTURE OF AI AGENTS
articles.summary: Using large foundation models for agent tool-calling is like using a Ferrari to deliver pizza - expensive, rate-limited, and overpowered for what's essentially a selection task. NVIDIA's research shows small language models are actually better suited for tool orchestration, and testing confirms this - a Salesforce xLAM model runs fast on a Mac M2 Pro with excellent accuracy while forcing better system design through deterministic interfaces and robust error handling.

articles.title: PALANTIR TOPS $1 BILLION IN REVENUE FOR THE FIRST TIME
articles.summary: Palantir exceeded Wall Street estimates, achieving over $1 billion in quarterly revenue for the first time and raising its full-year revenue guidance. The company forecasts Q3 revenue between $1.083 billion and $1.087 billion, surpassing analyst expectations. Significant growth was seen in U.S. revenues, with government contracts and commercial segments contributing to a 140% increase in total contract value to $2.27 billion.

articles.title: 25 PROVEN TACTICS TO ACCELERATE AI ADOPTION AT YOUR COMPANY
articles.summary: Companies like Shopify, Zapier, and Duolingo are using specific strategies to enhance AI adoption, focusing on organizational change rather than just technology. These strategies include explaining actionable AI tactics, tracking and rewarding AI usage, reducing approval bottlenecks for AI tools, and transforming AI enthusiasts into educators. Successful AI adoption also hinges on prioritizing high-impact tasks and integrating AI into daily workflows to drive productivity and innovation.

articles.title: THE EFFICIENCY PARADOX: WHY OVER-OPTIMIZING COULD KILL YOUR STARTUP
articles.summary: Frederick Taylor, the godfather of efficiency, failed spectacularly when he first optimized a machine shop - production dropped 30% because he perfected a system for yesterday's problems while the company was still figuring out what to build. Modern founders fall into the same trap, spending weeks implementing Google-style OKRs with ten customers, or A/B testing headlines while their business model is broken.

articles.title: HOW TO WIN WITH AI VIDEO ADS
articles.summary: Ad expert Dan Birdwhistell (who's managed $800M for Shopify and Coinbase) found AI video ads beat human ads by 2x on conversion - here's his playbook after 50+ hours with Google's Veo3.

articles.title: HATCH (PROGRAM)
articles.summary: Equal Ventures has launched a "Hatch" program to work with founders before they even incorporate.

articles.title: FLOWISE
articles.summary: Flowise is an open-source visual development platform for building AI agents, multi-agent systems, and LLM workflows.

articles.title: APPWRITE SITES
articles.summary: Appwrite Sites is an open-source platform for deploying and scaling static and server-rendered web applications with built-in domain management.

articles.title: YOU COULD JUST CHOOSE OPTIMISM
articles.summary: Two old men on separate flights both ordered champagne - one complained it wasn't good enough and spent the flight grouchy, the other was genuinely happy to be drinking free alcohol while flying over the Alps. As a founder, you face this choice daily: complain about investor rejections, bugs, and competition, or stay genuinely excited about building something new. Complainers hide behind "high standards", but choosing optimism doesn't mean accepting mediocrity - it means not letting every setback poison your energy, because in startups, your mood becomes your team's mood and eventually your company's culture.

articles.title: AI'S "JUST SHIP IT." PROBLEM
articles.summary: Stanford studied 100,000 developers and found AI boosts simple tasks by 60% but complex work by only 5%, with most gains around 20% - plus AI-generated code increases rework. The punchline is that AI makes you throw spaghetti at the wall faster, but it doesn't make your wall bigger or help you validate whether the spaghetti stuck - and when 75% of product ideas fail anyway, shipping yourself out of business just got easier.

articles.title: SHOULD YOU TAKE MONEY FROM CORPORATE INVESTMENT ARMS?
articles.summary: Stanford data shows corporate VCs now participate in over 20% of startup deals, offering patient capital, distribution channels, and even built-in exits - but they come with hidden strings. Unlike traditional VCs who reserve capital for follow-ons and help build syndicates, corporates rarely lead rounds, move slower with weeks of approvals, and often push toxic terms like ROFRs that scare off other acquirers. Another trap is when their strategic agenda conflicts with maximizing your value - they might block a great exit because the buyer is their competitor, or vanish overnight when corporate leadership changes.

articles.title: ANDY RACHLEFF EXPLAINS HIS PRODUCT PHILOSOPHY OF "SLUGGING PERCENTAGE, NOT BATTING AVERAGE"
articles.summary: While every other job rewards batting average, startups need a slugging percentage - take tons of shots knowing most will fail because the magnitude of your wins matters way more than how often you're right.

articles.title: GOLD DIGGERS
articles.summary: Some founders raise money to look for gold, others find the gold, then raise money to extract it - and you'd better be the second type.

articles.title: SHOULD VCS INVEST IN ‘JOCKEYS' OR ‘HORSES'?
articles.summary: VCs often debate whether to prioritize the founder or the business.
